Introduction
“The whole is greater than the sum of its parts.” This statement, 
commonly attributed to Aristotle, is surely manifested in the  
upper limb. Through the complex interplay of neuromusculoskel-
etal elements in the upper limb, activities as disparate as playing a 
piano and throwing a shotput can be achieved. The diversity of 
functional roles performed by the upper limb leads to a similar 
diversity in the multitude of injuries that can occur in this ana -
tomic region. Table 1.8 includes photos and additional descrip -
tions of many of the tests described in this chapter.
Upper Limb Physical Examination
Shoulder Special Tests
The anatomic and functional complexity of the shoulder requires 
that each physical exam maneuver be interpreted in the context  
of patient history, demographics, and activity/sporting level. The 
physical exam should be an extension of the medical history and 
should be performed considering how the response of each 
maneuver will advance the diagnostic conclusion.
Anterior Apprehension and Relocation Tests
These maneuvers test for anterior glenohumeral joint instability. 
The patient is placed in the supine position. The examiner abducts 
the shoulder and flexes the elbow to 90 degrees. The examiner then 
uses one hand to externally rotate the patient’s humerus using the 
patient’s forearm as the lever. At the same time, the examiner’s 
other hand is placed posterior to the patient’s proximal humerus 
and exerts an anteriorly directed force on the humeral head.  
The test is positive if the patient indicates a feeling of impending 
anterior dislocation. T o perform the relocation test, the examiner 
places a hand on the anterior humeral head and exerts a posteriorly 
directed force. The test is positive if the patient reports relief of 
apprehension.
77
Posterior Apprehension Test
This test evaluates posterior glenohumeral joint stability. The  
patient’s affected shoulder is forward flexed to 90 degrees and then 
maximally internally rotated. A posteriorly directed force is then 
placed in line with the humerus. If there the humeral head trans -
lates posteriorly by at least 50% or the patient feels apprehension, 
the test is positive.
77
Sulcus Sign
This test indicates inferior glenohumeral joint instability. The 
patient is seated or standing with the arm relaxed at the side. The 
examiner applies a traction force (gripping at either the elbow or 
forearm). If the patient has inferior laxity, a visible sulcus will be 
seen at the lateral deltoid, between the humeral head and the 
acromion.
77
O’Brien’s Test
This test assesses for both acromioclavicular (AC) joint and labral 
pathology. It consists of two positions. In position 1, the shoulder 
is flexed forward to 90 degrees with the elbow fully extended. The 
arm is then adducted 15 degrees, and the shoulder is internally 
rotated so that the patient’s thumb is pointing down. Ensure that 
the rotation originates from the humerus, not solely the forearm. 
The patient then resists the examiner’s downward force. In posi-
tion 2, the shoulder is then externally rotated so that the patient’s 
diagnoses where an expedient surgical referral is necessary, 
such as acute, full-thickness tears of the rotator cuff, pectoralis 
major, and distal biceps tendons, as well as scaphoid fracture 
and scapholunate dissociation.
• Special emphasis should be g iven to diagnostic entities where 
a breadth of pathology can impact the upper limb, such as the 
spectrum of rotator cuff diseases and shoulder instability.
36
Upper Limb Pain and Dysfunction
MICHAEL BARIA AND EDWARD LASKOWSKI
• T he upper extremity is anatomically complex and functionally 
dynamic, and therefore assessment of the local problem in the 
context of the kinetic chain is vital.
• T he physical examination should be interpreted in light of the 
patient’s demographics, activity, and focused history.
• Although most upper ex tremity pathology can be managed 
nonoperatively, special attention should be given to those

palm is facing up, and the patient again resists the downward 
force from the examiner. Pathology of the AC joint causes pain 
directly over the AC joint in both position 1 and 2. Glenoid labral 
pathology causes pain and/or clicking/displacement clicking in 
position 1, which is relieved in position 2. 95
Horizontal Adduction (Scarf) Test
This test traditionally is used to assess AC joint pathology. The 
shoulder is passively flexed to 90 degrees and then adducted across 
the chest (as if the patient is donning a scarf). Pain located at the 
AC joint suggests AC joint pathology.79
Speed’s Test
This test evaluates for proximal biceps tendon pathology. The 
shoulder is forward flexed to 90 degrees with the elbow extended 
and palm supinated. The examiner applies a downward force at 
the forearm against the patient’s active resistance. Pain in the 
region of the bicipital groove suggests proximal biceps tendon 
pathology.77
Impingement Testing
Neer-Walsh Maneuver. With the patient’s arm at the side, the 
shoulder is internally rotated. The shoulder is passively forward 
flexed to 180 degrees while maintaining internal rotation. Pain 
appreciated near full flexion in the subacromial area indicates 
rotator cuff involvement (in the acromiohumeral interval), which 
may include rotator cuff tendon disease.77
Hawkins-Kennedy Maneuver. The shoulder and elbow are 
simultaneously passively flexed to 90 degrees. The examiner then 
grasps the patient’s forearm, stabilizes the scapula, and uses the 
forearm as to internally rotate the glenohumeral joint. Pain indi -
cates rotator cuff involvement (in the acromiohumeral interval), 
which may include rotator cuff tendon disease.77
Drop Arm Test
The examiner passively abducts the patient’s shoulder 90 degrees 
in the scapular plane (usually about 10–20 degrees of adduction). 
The patient is asked to slowly lower the arm down. Inability to 
slowly lower the arm to the side, resulting in pain and a sudden 
drop of the arm, may indicate a high-grade rotator cuff tear or 
significant rotator cuff inflammation.77
Empty Can/Full Can Test
T raditionally, the empty can test is considered a gold standard 
physical exam maneuver for evaluation of rotator cuff tears.  
For this test, the shoulder is abducted to 90 degrees in the plane 
of the scapula, and the patient is instructed to rotate the thumb 
down (as if to empty a can). The aim of this rotation is to isolate 
the rotator cuff by reducing deltoid activation. The examiner  
then performs manual resistance, and pain plus weakness can  
indicate a rotator cuff tear. However, this exam maneuver has two 
major limitations. First, when turning the thumb down, many 
patients only rotate through the forearm; thus the deltoid activa -
tion is never reduced, and supraspinatus is not isolated. Second, 
if the patient does internally rotate at the shoulder, this causes 
impingement, increases pain, and may lead to an overdiagnosis of 
high-grade rotator cuff tears. For this reason, the authors recom -
mend the full can test, in which the same resistance is performed 
with the patient holding the thumb up (as if holding a full can). 
Electrophysiologic studies have shown no difference in supraspi -
natus activation between full and empty can positioning. 58,62,104 
Additionally, a full can reduces impingement-related pain and 
allows for a more precise examination of rotator cuff strength. 43
Hornblower’s Test
The patient flexes and adducts the shoulder with the elbow bent 
to 90 degrees as if blowing a horn or trumpet. The patient holds 
this position and resists the examiner’s attempt to internally rotate 
the shoulder. This external rotation force will test both supraspi -
natus and infraspinatus. Pain and weakness indicate a rotator cuff  
injury.26,49
Belly Press Test
This test assesses subscapularis injuries. The patient places the 
fingertips on the umbilicus and elevates both elbows forward. 
This movement is causing shoulder internal rotation while elimi -
nating pectoralis major involvement. A positive test is present 
when the patient’s elbow is unable to be brought forward.43
Gerber Lift-Off Test
This also tests the subscapularis because it mimics the move -
ments mentioned in the belly press test. The patient places a 
hand on own lower back and then attempts to push the hand 
posteriorly, effectively lifting the hand off the back. This is  
effective in isolating subscapularis, but care should be taken  
to observe pain inhibition in this position. This will provoke 
pain with many shoulder conditions and worsen impingement-
related symptoms, so all pain should not be attributed to sub -
scapularis pathology. 26,49
Elbow Special Tests
Cozen’s Test
This evaluates for common extensor tendinopathy (tennis elbow). 
With the elbow extended and forearm pronated, the patient 
actively extends the wrist against resistance. Lateral elbow pain 
suggests pathology at the common extensor tendon. 75
Mill’s Test
This also evaluates for tennis elbow. With a fully extended elbow, 
the wrist is passively flexed and maximally pronated, causing 
additional stretch of the common extensor tendon. Pain indicates 
pathology at the lateral elbow.120
Hook Test
This assesses for a distal biceps tendon rupture. With the elbow 
flexed to 90 degrees and forearm supinated, the patient performs 
an isometric contraction of the biceps muscle. The examiner then 
sweeps lateral to medial to assess if the distal biceps tendon is 
intact. Care should be taken not to confuse an intact lacertus 
fibrosus with the biceps tendon.88
Moving Valgus Stress
This assesses for ulnar collateral ligament (UCL) tears. With the 
patient seated or standing, the shoulder is abducted to 90 degrees, 
and the elbow is passively flexed to 120 degrees. The examiner then 
grabs the wrist and applies a posteriorly directed force to maximally 
externally rotate the shoulder. The posterior force is maintained, 
and the elbow is extended. Medial elbow pain between 60 and  
120 degrees is a positive test (see Table 36.2, later).42

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
Ligamentous Instability Test
The examiner flexes the patient’s elbow 20 to 30 degrees and sta-
bilizes the patient’s arm by placing one hand at the elbow and the 
other on the distal forearm. Varus and valgus forces are placed 
across the elbow by the examiner to test the stability of the radial 
and ulnar collateral ligaments, respectively.75
WRIST and Hand Special Tests
Finkelstein Test
This test is used to detect tenosynovitis of the extensor pollicis 
brevis and abductor pollicis longus tendons (de Quervain tenosy-
novitis). With the forearm in neutral position, the patient makes 
a fist with the thumb inside the fingers, and the examiner pas -
sively deviates the wrist in an ulnar direction. A positive test result 
causes pain in the distribution of the affected tendons. 76
Watson Test
This test assesses scapholunate stability. The patient’s wrist begins 
in an ulnarly deviated position. The examiner places a dorsally 
directed force against the proximal volar pole of the scaphoid. The 
examiner then radially deviates the wrist while continuing to 
place the same force against the scaphoid. A “pop” or subluxation 
of the scaphoid indicates a positive test result. 76
Rehabilitation Principles of Upper  
Limb Injury
The importance of making the correct diagnosis in planning  
an appropriate treatment program cannot be overemphasized.  
A complete diagnosis includes whether the injury is acute, chronic, 
or an acute exacerbation of a chronic injury. Understanding the 
mechanism of injury is important. Tissues that are overloaded, as 
well as those directly injured, must be identified. Functional bio -
mechanical deficits such as strength and flexibility imbalances are 
frequently present. The patient often tries to compensate for the 
injury by altering movement patterns and using muscle substitu -
tions, which impair effective force funneling in the kinetic chain, 
reduce functional performance, and contribute to secondary inju-
ries in the kinetic chain at sites distant from the site of pain.
Once an accurate diagnosis has been made with a thorough 
history, physical examination, and appropriate diagnostic testing, 
an effective treatment program can be developed. Kibler 61 has 
proposed three broad stages of rehabilitation: the acute stage, the 
recovery stage, and the functional stage. The acute stage of reha -
bilitation focuses on reducing the patient’s symptoms and facili -
tating tissue healing. In specific circumstances, immobilization 
through splinting or casting may be used during the acute stage 
of rehabilitation.
Protection, rest, ice, compression, and elevation (PRICE) is 
frequently used in the acute phase of rehabilitation. Rest should not 
be absolute. It is important for the patient to maintain cardiovascu-
lar fitness, strength, and flexibility during this phase. Core strength-
ening and aerobic conditioning should be emphasized. The patient 
should be instructed on appropriate activities that can be performed 
during the acute stage of rehabilitation that will not aggravate 
symptoms or be detrimental to tissue healing. For example, if a vol-
leyball player has rotator cuff tendinopathy, activities that could be 
done include passive glenohumeral joint range of motion (ROM) 
exercises to maintain flexibility and glenohumeral joint health, rid-
ing a stationary bicycle for cardiovascular fitness, and performing 
scapular stabilizing exercises in preparation for more advanced reha-
bilitative exercises of the shoulder. Kinetic chain deficits should be 
identified and “weak links” treated during the acute rehabilitation 
stage.5
During the acute phase of rehabilitation, cryotherapy can be 
used for acute injuries to decrease pain, inflammation, muscle 
guarding, and edema. 50,57 Heat increases blood flow, reduces 
muscle spasm, reduces pain, and can be used in the acute phase of 
rehabilitation of chronic injuries. 57 High-frequency electrical 
stimulation may also be used during the acute phase of rehabilita-
tion to reduce muscle guarding and increase local circulation. 132
Opioid and nonopioid analgesics may be required for pain 
control during the acute phase of rehabilitation. Nonsteroidal 
antiinflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are often used for their analgesic 
and antiinflammatory properties. Randomized placebo-controlled 
trials have demonstrated reduced pain, edema, tenderness, and a 
faster return to activity in NSAID-treated athletes than in those 
treated with placebo. 6,129 It is important to remember, however, 
that NSAIDs are not entirely benign and can cause significant 
gastrointestinal, renal, cardiovascular, hematologic, dermatologic, 
and neurologic side effects. 40,124,221 Because of these concerns, 
NSAIDs should be used judiciously.
Oral and injected corticosteroids have also been used for pain 
control and reduction of inflammation during the acute phase of 
rehabilitation. Because of the possibility of significant systemic 
and localized effects, the use of corticosteroids should be limited 
to select cases.32,112 Potential complications include suppression of 
the avascular necrosis, skin depigmentation, fat atrophy, infec -
tion, or tendon rupture.24
The patient can advance to the recovery phase of rehabilitation 
when pain has been adequately controlled and tissue healing has 
occurred.61 This is indicated by full pain-free ROM and the ability 
to participate in strengthening exercises for the injured limb. The 
emphasis of the recovery phase of rehabilitation involves the resto-
ration of flexibility, strength, and proprioception in the affected 
limb. Strength and flexibility imbalances, maladaptive movement 
patterns, and muscle substitutions should be corrected in this 
phase of rehabilitation. Open kinetic chain exercises can be benefi-
cial when strength imbalances are being corrected, whereas closed 
chain exercises are frequently used to provide joint stabilization 
through muscle cocontraction. Cardiovascular and general fitness 
should be maintained with progression to functional activities  
toward the end of this phase. Blood flow restriction training can be 
used to provide a strength stimulus for healing tissue at a low load 
and to start strength progression at an earlier stage.52a
The patient can begin the functional stage of rehabilitation when 
the injured limb has regained 75% to 80% of normal strength com-
pared with the uninjured limb and when there are no strength and 
flexibility imbalances.61 The patient’s rehabilitation must continue to 
address maladaptive movement patterns and muscle substitutions, 
and full strength should be obtained during this stage. Functional 
activities should be incorporated into the rehabilitation program 
with a vocational/avocational or sports-specific progression that 
eventually leads to a return to normal activities.
Musculoskeletal Problems of the Upper Limb
Conditions of the Shoulder
Acromioclavicular Joint Sprains
AC joint sprains account for 9% of all shoulder injuries and are 
most frequent in males in their third decade of life.79 Most injuries

occur as a result of direct trauma from a fall or blow to the acro -
mion. Physical examination demonstrates point tenderness, a 
positive horizontal adduction (Scarf sign) test, and pain in both 
positions 1 and 2 of O’Brien’s test.
AC joint sprains are classified into six types (Fig. 36.1).
Type 1: AC ligament sprain only with normal radiographs.
Type 2: AC ligament disruption but with intact coracoclavicular 
ligaments. Radiographs demonstrate clavicular elevation rela-
tive to the acromion but less than 25% displacement.
Type 3: Disruption of the AC and coracoclavicular ligaments, but 
the deltotrapezial fascia remains intact. Radiographs reveal a 
25% to 100% increase in the coracoclavicular interspace rela -
tive to the normal shoulder.
Type 4: Disruption of the coracoclavicular and AC ligaments, 
with posterior displacement of the distal clavicle into the tra -
pezius muscle.
Type 5: Disruption of coracoclavicular and AC ligaments and 
clavicular displacement through the deltotrapezial fascia. This 
results in an increase in the coracoclavicular interspace to 
greater than 100% of the normal shoulder.
Type 6: Disruption of the coracoclavicular and AC ligaments with 
inferior clavicular displacement below the coracoid process.
Radiographic evaluation of the AC joint should include 
anteroposterior and lateral views of the AC joint and a Zanca view 
(anteroposterior projection with 15 degrees cephalic tilt). Though 
commonly obtained in the past, stress views do not provide 
additional clinically useful information.

Type 1, 2, and 3 AC joint sprains are usually treated nonop -
eratively. A brief period of sling immobilization may be used to 
reduce pain. Indications for surgical intervention for type 3 
sprains include persistent pain and AC joint instability despite 
conservative care or unsatisfactory cosmetic results. Some authors 
advocate surgical treatment of type 3 sprains in athletes who par-
ticipate in sports that place a high demand on the upper 
limbs,
20,67,103 but the current literature favors nonoperative treat-
ment for type 3 sprains.121 Type 4, 5, and 6 sprains require surgi-
cal consultation.79
Biceps Pathology
The biceps muscle-tendon unit crosses both the shoulder and  
elbow joint. It originates from the superior glenoid-labral com -
plex and inserts at the radial tuberosity. It performs a variety  
of functions, including contributing to the stability and flexion  
of the glenohumeral joint, flexion of the elbow, and supination of 
the forearm. Because the biceps muscle tendon unit crosses two 
joints and confers both strength and stability to the upper limb, 
it is prone to injuries along its entirety.
28
Proximal Biceps Tendinopathy. The tendon of the long head 
of the biceps is part of the rotator interval and is critical for nor -
mal shoulder stability and biomechanics. The tendon can be dam-
aged by the same impingement that causes chronic degeneration 
of the rotator cuff, which is why proximal biceps tendinopathy 
often coexists with rotator cuff tendinopathy. The proximal biceps 
tendon is also commonly injured by repetitive traction forces, 
such as overhead throwing. Patients with chronic proximal biceps 
tendinopathy often present with atraumatic anterior shoulder 
pain that is exacerbated by reaching overhead and throwing. They 
will be tender in the bicipital groove and have pain reproduced 
with Speed test. Because the proximal tendon originates from the 
superior labrum, O’Brien test may also provoke shoulder pain. 
However, pain originating from the biceps tendon localizes to the 
anterior shoulder (rather than deeper in the joint) and is not usu-
ally associated with mechanical symptoms. T reatment for proxi -
mal biceps tendinopathy includes optimization of shoulder  
mechanics, restoration of normal scapular motion to relieve sub-
acromial impingement, and shoulder proprioceptive training. 
Biceps tendon sheath injections may be a useful adjunct. Patients 
with refractory symptoms may benefit from surgical tenotomy/
tenodesis.
19,82
Long Head of Biceps Tendon Rupture.  The proximal biceps 
tendon may also be injured by an acute rupture. This can occur 
with or without trauma. In the absence of trauma, the patient is 
typically older and has a history of chronic rotator cuff disease. 
T rauma, such as a fall or catching a heavy object, can rupture the 
proximal biceps tendon. Patients with a proximal biceps tendon 
rupture will present with a classic Popeye deformity, in which the 
muscle belly will retract toward the elbow and result in a visible 
asymmetry. Most proximal biceps tendon ruptures are treated 
nonoperatively. Functionally, a proximal rupture results in only 
10% loss of elbow flexion strength (because of the greater contri-
butions from brachialis and brachioradialis). If the patient must 
lift heavier loads for work or sport, a tenodesis may be performed 
to optimize function.
28
Distal Biceps Tendon Rupture. This usually result from sudden 
eccentric load (i.e., catching a heavy object while pulling up in 
elbow flexion). Heavy deadlifting is another common sporting 
event implicated in this injury because the tendon is fully extended 
and then placed under significant eccentric force during the initial 
pulling phase of the lift. Patients will describe sudden deep pain,

usually with a “pop” deep in the elbow. Ecchymosis and a visible 
deformity are observable (as the muscle retracts proximally). Distal 
biceps tendon rupture can be diagnosed on physical examination 
by a positive Hook test. In some cases, a fully ruptured distal ten-
don can still feel intact because it is held in place by the lacertus 
fibrosus. In the setting of a concerning injury, additional testing 
should include resisted elbow flexion and resisted forearm supina-
tion. Pain and weakness with these movements should raise the 
suspicion for a high-grade distal tendon injury and prompt addi -
tional evaluation with MRI. If a distal rupture is confirmed, a 
timely surgical referral is key because fixation is required for opti-
mal strength, function, and cosmesis. Delays in surgical care are 
often complicated by significant fibrosis.
55
Rotator Cuff Tendon Disease
The rotator cuff (comprised of the supraspinatus, infraspinatus, 
teres minor, and subscapularis) is impacted by a large spectrum  
of pathology. The primary pathologies include (1) tendinopathy 
without significant tearing, (2) partial tears, (3) chronic full-
thickness tears, and (4) acute, traumatic full-thickness tears. 
Though the nuances of each are unique, they are most easily cat -
egorized according to their primary treatment method. As such, 
we will discuss them in the following categories: nonsurgical 
(tendinosis, partial tears, and chronic full-thickness tears) and 
surgical (acute full-thickness tears).
Primary Treatment: Nonsurgical. T endinopathy, partial tears, 
and chronic full-thickness tears are best treated first with a non-
surgical, rehabilitation-based treatment plan. Surgery should only 
be considered for these ailments after patients fail comprehensive 
nonoperative treatment.
The etiology of these ailments is multifactorial, and contribut-
ing factors include overuse, poor biomechanics, microtrauma, 
minor injuries, and a genetic predisposition. These conditions are 
often chronic because of the poor healing capacity of the rotator 
cuff. Microvascular studies demonstrated a hypovascular zone on 
the articular surface of the leading edge of the supraspinatus  
tendon.
74,105 This region has been implicated in rotator cuff   
degeneration and correlates with the higher incidence of articular-
sided, partial-thickness tears in the anterior aspect of the supraspi-
natus tendon.
Role of Impingement in the Development of Chronic Rotator 
Cuff Dysfunction. The impingement of the rotator cuff can be 
external or internal. External impingement is more commonly 
referred to as subacromial impingement. This occurs when the 
arm is raised overhead, and the greater tuberosity enters the sub-
acromial space. If the patient has poor scapulothoracic mechanics, 
the dyskinetic movement does not allow for normal clearance of 
the subacromial space. The rotator cuff then becomes impinged, 
and chronic frictional damage occurs. This impingement must be 
addressed during rehabilitation to prevent ongoing injury.
73
Internal impingement is less common. It occurs overhead in 
athletes when the arm is abducted and externally rotated. If there 
are proprioceptive deficiencies or subtle increases in shoulder lax-
ity, this can lead to contact and friction between the external rota-
tors and the posterior glenoid. With increased laxity, the humeral 
head translates anteriorly, which accentuates the impingement.
7,52
Clinical Presentation. Shoulder impingement patients will pres-
ent a variety of historical scenarios: They may be entirely atrau -
matic, with gradual onset of symptoms; they may have an injury 
mechanism, such as catching a heavy falling object; or there may 
be a new (or more intense) activity, such as a vigorous exercise 
routine, or more consistent or higher velocity overhead throwing.
52
Symptomatically, patients note anterolateral shoulder and lat -
eral arm pain exacerbated by overhead activity, reaching behind 
the back, lifting, and lateral side lying on the affected side. Symp-
toms such as stiffness, weakness, and catching/clicking may be 
present.
52
On examination, observe for scapular dyskinesis (an upward, 
outwards rotation of the scapula with overhead arm movement 
caused by scapular instability) because this may be the founda -
tional cause of impingement. Positive impingement testing in the 
absence of trauma is also useful for establishing impingement as a 
contributing factor. Examiners should also screen for significant 
ROM restriction. Rotator cuff dysfunction can lead to mild ROM 
deficits, usually from pain avoidance and disuse, and most com-
monly involving decreased internal rotation. However, inability to 
abduct beyond 90 degrees and restricted external rotation indicate 
either adhesive capsulitis or advanced osteoarthritis (OA) rather 
than rotator cuff disease. Rotator cuff strength testing consists of 
full can, drop arm, hornblower, belly press, and Gerber lift-off test. 
Weakness with one or more of these should raise suspicion of sig-
nificant tendinopathy or a higher grade tear. Specifically, weakness 
with full can, drop arm, and hornblower tests usually indicates 
injury to the supraspinatus. Weakness with resisted external rota-
tion may indicate injury to supraspinatus or infraspinatus. A posi-
tive belly press and/or Gerber lift-off test indicates subscapularis 
injury.
When performing these rotator cuff special tests, care should 
be taken to distinguish between pain and pain-limited weakness 
vs. weakness caused by high-grade injury. Pain and pain-limited 
weakness typically indicate tendinosis or a partial tear. Significant 
weakness in the absence of a major injury may indicate a chronic, 
degenerative, full-thickness rotator cuff tear.
60
Radiographic evaluation of patients with suspected rotator cuff 
pathology should include anteroposterior, supraspinatus outlet, 
and axillary radiographs. Anteroposterior radiographs should be 
performed in the neutral, external, and internal rotation positions 
to adequately visualize the glenohumeral joint and the greater and 
lesser tuberosities. Full-thickness rotator cuff tears may demon
-
strate a narrow acromiohumeral distance of less than 7 mm and 
sclerosis on the undersurface of the acromion. This finding occurs 
because, in the absence of the supraspinatus, the deltoid contracts 
unopposed, causing superior humeral head migration. This pro-
cess takes time to develop and should not be relied upon to assess 
for an acute full-thickness injury. The supraspinatus outlet view 
allows for categorization of the acromion type (Fig. 36.2) and will 
reveal AC joint osteophytes. Ultrasound and MRI have higher

T reatment Approach. Because tendinosis leads to partial ten -
don tears, it is self-evident why these entities are grouped together 
for treatment. It may initially seem counterintuitive that a chronic 
full-thickness tear would undergo a similar treatment approach. 
However, a chronic tear of significant magnitude of one or mul -
tiple rotator cuff tendons is inoperable because the retracted tissue 
undergoes fatty infiltration and fibrosis. Without a possible direct 
repair, a restorative surgery does not exist.65,128 Consequently, the 
same treatment approach is used for partial tears or chronic full-
thickness tears of the rotator cuff.
Rehabilitation-based care should focus on pain reduction, cor-
rection of biomechanical abnormalities, and restoration of func -
tion. In the acute rehabilitation stage, emphasis should be on 
scapular stabilizers, rather than the rotator cuff itself, and establish-
ment of a balanced shoulder force couple. Specifically, strengthen-
ing muscles that stabilize (e.g., the serratus anterior), retract, and 
depress the scapula (e.g., rhomboids and inferior trapezius) and 
stretching muscles that protract and elevate the scapula (e.g., pec-
toralis minor, upper trapezius, and levator scapulae) can help  
to mechanically reduce impingement by establishing anterior-
posterior muscle balance and reducing forward shoulder protrac -
tion. Restoring normal scapulothoracic mechanics will alleviate 
functional impingement and reduce mechanical strain of the rota-
tor cuff. Next, direct strengthening of the rotator cuff should be  
implemented. Closed chain exercises can help to promote stability 
and proprioception, while open chain exercises can be used to 
correct strength imbalances, such as disproportionate weakness of 
the external rotators. If pain persists or interferes with therapeutic 
exercise, work, or sleep, a subacromial corticosteroid injection may 
be helpful.48,83,89
Primary Treatment: Surgical
In contrast to the rotator cuff pathologies discussed earlier, acute 
traumatic full-thickness tears should be referred for surgical con -
sultation to optimize their repair potential. Acute, full-thickness 
injuries are distinct and more urgent because, if left unrepaired, 
the patient will often end up with significant disability of the  
upper limb. Because of the unopposed deltoid contraction, the 
humeral head migrates superiorly, obliterating the normal sub -
acromial space and resulting in rotator cuff arthropathy, which is 
a unique pattern of advanced degenerative joint disease. This can 
be prevented by expedited rotator cuff repair.128
Physicians should maintain a high index of suspicion for an 
acute, full-thickness rotator cuff tear in patients who sustained a 
significant injury, such as a fall on an outstretched hand (FOOSH) 
or suddenly catching a heavy object. These patients will experience 
severe, sudden pain and significant functional decline, often result-
ing in inability to use the affected shoulder. These patients should 
be promptly evaluated with an MRI and surgical consultation. 
Even a delay as little as 6 weeks will result in fatty infiltration of the 
muscle and decrease the chances of a successful surgical outcome.
Glenohumeral Joint Instability
Glenohumeral joint stability is provided by static and dynamic 
stabilizers. The static stabilizers include the inferior, middle, and 
superior glenohumeral ligaments, as well as the articular surface 
and labrum.1 The dynamic stabilizers include the rotator cuff and 
the long head of biceps.68 The rotator cuff contributes to dynamic 
stability through a number of mechanisms. Concave compression, 
first described by Lippitt et al., 71 refers to the compressive forces 
placed on the glenohumeral joint during rotator cuff activation. 
These forces center the humeral head into the glenoid, helping 
resist joint translation. Because the glenohumeral ligaments are lax 
in the midranges of glenohumeral joint motion, coordinated rota-
tor cuff contraction and concave compression are vital mechanisms 
for stability.68 The distal rotator cuff tendon insertions are interwo-
ven with the joint capsule. This then allows for capsular tightening 
during rotator cuff activation, further accentuating the ability of 
the cuff-capsule complex to improve joint stability.68
When these stabilizing structures are compromised, the result-
ing instability is characterized in two large categories: traumatic/
unidirectional and atraumatic/multidirectional.
Traumatic Instability
T raumatic joint dislocations occur either in the anterior or the 
posterior direction. Classically, a traumatic, anteroinferior disloca-
tion occurs with high-velocity energy to the upper limb when  
the shoulder is abducted, extended, and externally rotated. In  
this position, the inferior glenohumeral ligament is maximally 
stretched, and the supramaximal force results in ligament tearing 
and joint dislocation. An example of this injury is when a defen -
sive football player is attempting to make a tackle but is out of 
position and uses the arm only to stop the runner. The clinical 
presentation is obvious with pain, and the patient is usually able 
to self-diagnose. On examination, there is visible deformity, with 
the humeral head sitting anterior. This may give the appearance of 
shoulder swelling. First, a neurovascular examination should be 
performed and documented to ensure that a limb-threatening 
injury is not present. Palpation of the proximal humerus should 
also be performed to rule out a humeral fracture (especially before 
an attempt at reduction). If the examination confirms a simple 
joint dislocation, one or two attempts at reduction should be 
made. A variety of reduction techniques have been described. 29 
They all attempt to relax the shoulder, minimize muscle spasm, 
and create enough axial traction that will allow the joint to  
reduce. This should always be done gently and slowly to avoid an 
iatrogenic injury, which has been documented in more forceful 
reduction attempts. If reduction attempts fail, the patient should 
be sent to the emergency room for consideration of reduction 
under sedation. In some cases, patients may present to clinic days 
after the dislocation and reduction. In this scenario, they will have 
a positive apprehension and relocation test. Postreduction radio -
graphs should be obtained to document a successful reduction as 
well as evaluate for bony injury. Radiographs may show a Hill-
Sachs lesion (posterolateral humeral head depression from direct 
impaction) or a bony Bankart lesion (anteroinferior glenoid frac -
ture). MRI is indicated for a patient with a history of multiple 
dislocations, presence of bony abnormality on x-ray, or when the 
clinical presentation suggests a concomitant high-grade rotator 
cuff tear.4
T reatment for a first-time traumatic dislocation that is  
uncomplicated (without significant bony pathology or rotator 
cuff injury) is generally a rehabilitation-based approach. Acutely, 
the focus is on pain reduction, a brief period of immobilization 
(#7 days), and progressive proprioceptive and strengthening in 
safe zones. Early strength should consist primarily of pain-free 
closed chain movements to foster joint stability and emphasize 
muscular cocontraction to provide dynamic stability. As the  
patient recovers, open chain exercises that further challenge pro -
prioception can be integrated. Surgical intervention is considered 
for patients who have multiple dislocations or who have bony/
soft tissue injuries that significantly increase the risk of recurrent

Epidemiologically, younger patients, especially those under age 
19 years, are at significant risk for repeat dislocations. 4,46
Posterior acute dislocations may occur after seizure or electrocu-
tion. Postdislocation sequelae include a posterior glenoid labrum 
tear (a reverse Bankart lesion) or a reverse Hill-Sachs lesion, an 
impaction fracture of the anterior humeral head.9,105 Seizure-related 
posterior instability can become chronically unstable if the patient 
has frequent seizures. The combination of forceful muscle contrac-
tion plus recurrent soft tissue and bony damage can lower the 
threshold for each subsequent dislocation. These patients may 
benefit from surgical stabilization, but long-term stability can still 
be compromised if the underlying seizure disorder is not well  
controlled.102
Atraumatic Instability
The other distinct category of instability is atraumatic and multi-
directional. This typically occurs in patients with hypermobility 
that impacts many joints. Patients often complain of pain that is 
difficult to localize, and they can voluntary sublux the joint (i.e., 
cause partial translation that is palpable to the patient and physi -
cian without full dislocation). They demonstrate laxity in all direc-
tions, and this often impacts both shoulders. Hallmark findings on 
the physical examination include visible voluntary subluxation,  
a high Beighton (assessment screen for hypermobility) score, and  
a positive sulcus sign. These patients should be managed with a  
rehabilitation-based approach. The patient should be educated to 
not voluntarily sublux the joint; cognitive behavioral therapy may 
be helpful to aid in diminishing this habit. Strengthening should 
occur in safe planes and ROMs that do not aggravate laxity.  
Finally, improving proprioception will help to further improve 
joint symptoms. Because these patients have increased laxity in 
connective tissue, surgical correction (often done through capsular 
plication) often has poor outcomes.114
The physical examination of an acute shoulder dislocation 
depends on the clinical setting. If an acute dislocation is wit -
nessed, as mentioned, a focused neurovascular exam should be 
performed to rule out a more worrisome injury. Additionally, 
palpation and ROM should be done to evaluate for a fracture that 
would preclude reduction in the field. If the patient is seen in the 
clinic postreduction, the examination should focus on evaluating 
for clinical instability with apprehension testing and special tests 
for labrum and/or rotator cuff injuries.
The most common radiographic evaluation includes the  
anteroposterior (AP), axillary, and scapular Y views. 14 The AP 
view visualizes osseous structures. 115 The AP view can be aug -
mented with internal rotation, which evaluates for a Hill-Sachs 
defect.115 The scapular Y and axillary views assess for active and 
reduced dislocations as well as glenoid fractures.115 Other special-
ized views include the Garth view and the West Point view, both 
of which are useful in the detection of Bankart fractures. The 
Stryker Notch view can be used for evaluation of Hill-Sachs  
defects and stress views for the documentation of the degree of 
glenohumeral joint instability (though the latter is not routinely 
necessary).115 MRI provides the best assessment for soft tissue 
injury, including the labrum, rotator cuff, and HAGL lesions, all 
of which may require surgical intervention. 
Overall Treatment
The treatment options for glenohumeral joint instability and  
dislocation include nonoperative and operative approaches.  
After initial glenohumeral joint subluxation episodes and in  
patients with multidirectional instability or unidirectional poste -
rior or inferior instability, a comprehensive rehabilitation program 
that addresses kinetic chain deficits, scapulothoracic mechanics, 
shoulder girdle strength, flexibility, and neuromuscular control is 
appropriate. Operative intervention should be considered only in 
those patients who have failed to improve after a comprehensive 
nonoperative treatment program.107
For patients with glenohumeral joint instability, the strengthen-
ing program should begin with closed chain exercises that promote 
cocontraction of the glenohumeral joint–stabilizing musculature. 
The patient can eventually progress to functional open chain exer-
cises as stability is achieved. Proprioceptive closed and open chain 
exercises are also important to establish coordinated firing of the 
stabilizing shoulder girdle musculature and to attain neuromuscu-
lar engrams that respond appropriately to a dynamically changing 
environment.
For patients who have sustained a first-time traumatic anterior 
glenohumeral joint dislocation, the decision between a trial of 
nonoperative treatment vs. immediate surgical stabilization is 
more controversial. In the older, less active patient, nonoperative 
management is frequently successful.3 In the younger, more active 
patient involved in contact sports, studies have shown a very high 
repeat dislocation rate in those treated nonoperatively compared 
with those receiving early operative intervention.10,12,63,122
Glenoid labral tears. The glenoid labrum is a static stabilizer 
of the glenohumeral joint. Given the shallow conformation of the 
glenoid, the labrum provides increased contact surface and stabil-
ity for the humeral head. The labrum may be injured because of 
repetitive movements/overuse, a traumatic injury, or may undergo 
degenerative changes.
Overuse Labral Pathology. Classically, overhead throwing ath-
letes can injure the labrum because of the repeated forces through 
the joint. The late cocking and early acceleration phases of throw-
ing lead to increased torsional force from the biceps anchor on the 
labrum.15 This is referred to as the “peel-back” mechanism, and 
this chronic torsion can lead to labral injuries such as tear of the 
superior labrum from anterior to posterior. 33 Additionally, the 
follow-through phase increases traction forces from the biceps to 
the labrum. Athletes with labral tears may feel deep shoulder pain 
with or without mechanical symptoms (clicking or shifting). They 
may also complain of loss of control over their throws, or a sensa-
tion of “dead arm,” which manifests as arm heaviness or fatigue 
after throwing. Dead arm may be confused with cervical spine 
injuries, such as a stinger, because of the pain referral pattern.  
On examination, patients may demonstrate a loss of glenohu -
meral ROM (glenohumeral internal rotation deficit [GIRD])  
because of a posterior capsular contracture. They also may have a 
positive O’Brien, crank, and/or dynamic labral shear test. Patients 
should undergo comprehensive rehabilitation, including throw -
ing mechanics analysis, optimization of hip, spine, and shoulder 
mobility (including addressing their GIRD), and proper kinetic 
chain integration. If patients fail to improve with rehabilitation, 
they may benefit from surgical consultation.51
Posterior labral tears can also be the result of chronic, posteri -
orly directed forces. This is most often seen in athletes such as 
football linemen. As they engage their block with extended arms, 
the chronic posterior force causes degenerative labral damage. 
This results in subtle posterior translation during activity. Patients 
with posterior labral tears may complain of posterior pain when 
they sustain a posterior axial force. On examination, they may 
have pain with the posterior apprehension test. Rehabilitation of

Traumatic Labral T ears.  These are typically the result of 
shoulder dislocations. As the humeral head dislocates, it disrupts 
the labral tissue. These injuries are referred to as a Bankart lesion 
(after an anterior shoulder dislocation) and reverse Bankart lesion 
(after a posterior dislocation). Labral injuries increase the risk  
of a first-time dislocation developing chronic instability. The 
assessment of these injuries occurs in the context and timing of 
the shoulder dislocation evaluation. If patients present far after 
their dislocation, the examination may have positive apprehension 
testing, as well as positive O’Brien, crank, and dynamic labral 
shear tests. T reatment of these concomitant injuries is detailed in 
the shoulder instability section.
Degenerative Labral T ears. These are common in an aging 
population. They are associated with rotator cuff dysfunction. 
These are often found incidentally on MRIs performed for another 
reason. Degenerative labral tears are typically asymptomatic. In the  
absence of a proper mechanism of injury or objective instability, 
these findings do not warrant surgery and respond well to reha -
bilitation focused on a balanced shoulder force couple.
Adhesive Capsulitis
Adhesive capsulitis, or “frozen shoulder,” is characterized by a 
painful, progressively stiff shoulder in all motion planes with 
normal radiographs (which rule out OA).
41,91 Adhesive capsulitis 
occurs in approximately 2% to 5% of the population, is more 
common in females, and is most frequently seen between ages  
40 and 60 years.
18,23 It is frequently associated with diabetes  
mellitus, systemic inflammatory disorders, prolonged immobili -
zation (in the setting of medical events such as a stroke or pro -
longed surgical positioning), and thyroid disease. Histopathology 
is characterized by synovitis, fibroblastic proliferation, and the 
formation of nodular bands.
18
Adhesive capsulitis progresses in stages, of which there are 
three or four, depending on the classification system being refer -
enced (Table 36.1).45 Regardless of nomenclature, recognition of 
the clinical presentation is most important for optimal treatment. 
Early in the disease course (approximately the first 1–3 months), 
patients present with painful shoulder movements but with  
preserved ROM. Because the hallmark stiffness is absent, this  
is easy to misinterpret as rotator cuff pathology. Therefore any 
patient early in symptom presentation who has risk factors as 
noted should be educated on symptom progression and to return 
to clinic if stiffness develops. Next, the shoulder progresses into 
the “freezing stage.” This occurs between 3 and 9 months, is  
associated with persistent and worsening pain, and a significant 
amount of ROM is lost. Often, patients lose the ability to reach 
overhead and can no longer perform daily self-care, such as dress-
ing or washing their hair. During the frozen stage, symptoms 
usually have been present for 9 to 15 months, pain begins to  
recede (as the inflammatory process resolves), but the stiffness 
persists. Finally, with the “thawing” stage, stiffness improves for 
up to 2 to 3 years after initial symptom onset.
The physical and radiographic evaluation of these patients is 
straightforward. Given the appropriate history, patients will dem-
onstrate a progressive loss of ROM in all planes, with inability to 
abduct the shoulder beyond 90 degrees, along with a significant 
loss of external rotation. External rotation is the first movement 
to be restricted, so this should be an evaluation focus of any early 
presentation. The radiographic evaluation is often normal, which 
rules out OA as a cause of pain and stiffness. The progression of 
stiffness in adhesive capsulitis is also more precipitous than is 
typically observed with OA, which is far more gradual. Advanced 
imaging is usually unnecessary, but MRI will demonstrate signifi-
cant capsular and synovial thickening and inflammation.
During the initial and freezing stages, treatment consists of 
deep heat modalities, analgesics, and efforts to restore ROM. Pain 
severity may interfere with ROM exercises; therefore up to three 
intraarticular corticosteroid injections can be used to treat inflam-
mation and pain, facilitate rehabilitation, and hasten the time to 
resolution.
45,117 Once adequate pain control is achieved, joint 
mobilization along with passive and active assisted ROM exercises 
should be initiated. As the patient’s symptoms improve, active 
ROM activities can be added, along with open chain and proprio-
ceptive exercises. Integration of active ROM should be done as 
soon as possible to correct any maladaptive movement patterns 
that have developed.
If the patient presents later in the frozen stage, steroid injec -
tions do not have the same potential benefit because fibrosis has 
already developed. A steroid injection in this phase still may help 
with pain, but it does not seem to change the disease course. Some 
studies indicate that steroid injections given early in the course of 
the condition can hasten recovery of ROM.
90
Most patients will have restoration of normal function over a 
12- to 14-month period.44,86 In patients who do not improve after 
6 months of nonoperative treatment, other treatments aimed at 
resolving fibrotic tissue can be employed. These include capsular 
hydrodistention, manipulation under anesthesia, and arthroscopic 
lysis of adhesions and capsular release. 17,18,31,36,57,63,92,97,99,132 
Finally, a growing body of literature supports the use of platelet-
rich plasma (PRP) injections for long-term symptom improvement 
(compared with steroid and physical therapy).
106
Pectoralis Major Ruptures
The pectoralis major muscle is responsible for arm flexion, adduc-
tion, and medial rotation. It has distinct sternal and clavicular heads 
that, as they transition at their myotendinous junction, rotate on 
each other as they insert on the lateral aspect of the bicipital groove. 
Injuries to the pectoralis major are rare but must be recognized early 
because of the potential for significant impairment.

Pectoralis major injuries usually occur at the distal tendon 
because of a large eccentric force. Classically, this occurs during 
bench press. The patient will feel pain and a pop. Extensive  
ecchymosis develops in the subsequent hours along with signifi -
cant muscle deformity. On examination, the ecchymosis is 
prominent, and there will be a loss of the normal deltoid-pectoral 
contour. The pectoralis contour will essentially be vertical,  
instead of oriented toward the biceps groove, because the muscle 
has retracted. There will be pain and weakness with manual 
muscle testing of the pectoralis major muscle. 111,133
The diagnosis can be made clinically without further imaging. 
MRI may be obtained for confirmation and surgical planning. 
The patient should be promptly referred to an orthopedic surgeon 
because delayed repair will be complicated by advanced scarring.
Conditions of the Elbow
Lateral Epicondylosis (or Epicondylitis)
Lateral epicondylosis is a common tendinopathy of the elbow and 
is frequently referred to as tennis elbow. This malady dates back 
to 1883, when it was described as an injury resulting from lawn 
tennis,27 and it is reported to occur in up to 50% of tennis  
players.94 Any activity that places repetitive stress on the lateral 
forearm musculature, however, can cause this condition. It is 
more common in patients older than 35 years of age and peaks in 
those between ages 40 and 50 years. 56,93 It is more common in 
male than in female tennis players, but does not display a sex 
predilection in the general population.25
The hallmark pathologic change is angiofibroblastic dysplasia, 
which represents a degenerative (not inflammatory) condition.93,108 
This primarily impacts the extensor carpi radialis brevis, but also 
involves the extensor digitorum communis origin in 30% of 
cases.93 The origins of the extensor carpi radialis longus or extensor 
carpi ulnaris are rarely involved.
Patients report gradual symptom onset, which is provoked  
after specific gripping activities. The backhand swing in tennis 
frequently exacerbates pain, as does gripping, repetitive wrist  
extension, and forearm pronation and supination. On physical 
examination, patients have tenderness on palpation of the lateral 
epicondyle. They also have positive Cozen and Mill tests. Entrap-
ment of the posterior interosseous branch of the radial nerve 
mimics lateral epicondylosis, but the tenderness in this condition 
is 3 to 4 cm distal to the lateral epicondyle (at the level of the 
radial tunnel/supinator).130
In the proper clinical context, radiographs do not need to be 
routinely performed. If there is diagnostic uncertainty, plain films 
assist in evaluating for elbow OA, a radial neck fracture (if the patient 
had a fall or other trauma), or calcific deposit in the tendons.25
T reatment should emphasize proper work ergonomics, sport-
ing technique, and corrective strengthening. Ensuring proper 
racquet grip size and string tension is essential. Eccentric strength-
ening of the wrist extensors appears to be the most effective exer -
cise intervention. 126 Peritendinous corticosteroid injections are 
occasionally used, but their long-term efficacy is questionable. 8,70 
Additionally, this region is superficial, so complications such as 
subcutaneous fat atrophy and skin depigmentation are seen more 
often with steroid injections. Other injectables, such as autolo -
gous blood and PRP injections, have been used. 31,84,87 Recent 
investigations have demonstrated superior efficacy of PRP and 
autologous blood injection compared with corticosteroid. 11,38 
Extracorporeal shock wave therapy has been reported to be suc -
cessful in 48% to 73% of cases recalcitrant to other nonoperative 
measures.96 Recalcitrant cases can also be treated with surgical 
debridement of the degenerative tissue, with approximately 85% 
of surgically treated patients reporting good to excellent results 
and only 3% reporting no improvement.30,94
Medial Epicondylosis (or Epicondylitis)
Medial epicondylosis, also known as golfer’s elbow, has the  
same degenerative pathologic changes as those described in lateral 
epicondylopathy.98 The degenerative changes most commonly 
affect the pronator teres and flexor carpi radialis. 98 Risk factors 
include training errors, faulty equipment, and repetitive activities 
requiring wrist flexion and forearm pronation.
Patients typically report a gradual onset of pain over the medial 
epicondyle provoked by repetitive gripping, wrist flexion, and 
forearm pronation. The patient may report grip strength weak -
ness. Physical examination demonstrates tenderness to palpation 
over the medial epicondyle, pain when a tight fist is made, and 
pain with resisted wrist flexion and forearm pronation.
Oblique radiographs of the medial epicondyle can reveal punc-
tuate calcifications in the region of the flexor tendon origins. 25  
It is also important to rule out degenerative changes in the poste-
rior medial aspect of the olecranon because this condition can 
cause symptoms similar to those seen with medial epicondylosis.
Nonoperative treatment should include discontinuation of 
aggravating activities and use of analgesic medications, physical 
modalities, bracing (e.g., medial counterforce strap and neutral 
wrist splint) for pain control, along with correcting kinetic chain 
deficits and training errors. Eccentric strengthening of the flexor-
pronator group is the standard of care. Corticosteroid injections 
have been used to treat this condition but can hasten degeneration; 
therefore clear goals regarding the role of corticosteroid injections 
should be established early in the treatment course. Autologous 
blood, PRP injections, and shock wave therapy may be useful 
adjuncts.96,125 Operative treatment is reserved for those who fail to 
improve with conservative measures.
Olecranon Bursitis
The olecranon bursa lies superficially over the olecranon process. 
Olecranon bursitis can be aseptic or septic. Aseptic bursitis is the result 
of acute hemorrhagic bursitis from trauma (e.g., fall or direct blow), 
repetitive microtrauma (e.g., leaning on the elbow), or gouty tophi.66 
The diagnosis is typically self-evident, with swelling and pain over the 
olecranon process. T raumatic bursitis will have a distinct injury, 
sudden onset, and signs of hemorrhage (warmth and erythema).
The treatment for traumatic and atraumatic bursitis is the same. 
PRICE principles are the foundation. Gentle compression with a 
compressive wrap or sleeve will reduce swelling. Avoidance of leaning 
on hard surfaces is crucial for eliminating continual irritation. For 
those who may have difficulty avoiding direct pressure, such as 
wheelchair-based patients, an elbow pad can be used for protection. 
Cryotherapy and NSAIDs may also be useful for pain control. Aspi-
ration should be avoided unless the patient fails a dedicated trial of 
the above because the tissue is so superficial, and the risk of iatrogenic 
infection is high (up to 10%). Aspiration with concomitant steroid 
injections should be avoided because steroids further heighten the 
infection risk. If aspiration is performed, injection with a sclerosant 
(doxycycline) may reduce recurrence of swelling and have antimicro-
bial protective effects. Prophylactic antibiotics that cover for common 
skin flora may also be prescribed.47 Surgical excision is reserved for 
patients who do not obtain relief with these measures.
The presence of inflammatory findings on examination should 
always raise the possibility of septic bursitis. Often, this can be

treatment. Patients likely will need to be admitted through the 
local emergency room for this therapy.
Ulnar Collateral Ligament Injuries
The UCL is composed of an anterior, posterior, and transverse 
bundle. The anterior bundle provides the majority of stability, 
resisting valgus force when the elbow is flexed.
127 Injuries to the 
UCL of the elbow are a result of acute or repetitive valgus stress.
Possibly the most recognizable sporting scenario for UCL  
injuries is throwing in baseball. A detailed description of throwing 
mechanics is found in the sports medicine chapter of this text 
(Chapter 40). In brief, valgus stress is highest in the late cocking 
and acceleration phases.
131 If that stress chronically overloads the 
UCL, degenerative changes occur. This results in chronic medial 
elbow pain during and after throwing. As degenerative changes 
accumulate, an acute-on-chronic injury may occur with a sudden 
pain and pop during a throw. In addition to medial elbow pain, 
the athlete may also experience ulnar nerve dysesthesias caused by 
traction and possible subluxation over the medial epicondyle. On 
examination, patients may demonstrate a 5-degree elbow flexion 
contracture, UCL tenderness, and pain during UCL stress testing 
(particularly the moving valgus stress test). Patients may also dem-
onstrate ulnar subluxation with repeated flexion and extension.
35
Anteroposterior, lateral, and oblique radiographs of the elbow 
should be obtained. Calcification of the UCL can occur, and avul-
sions from the humeral or ulnar attachments should be ruled 
out.
116 MRI can differentiate partial- vs. full-thickness injuries 
and can identify which bands are involved. Ultrasound has the 
added advantage that dynamic laxity can be assessed.
The treatment of UCL injuries is controversial, and no clear 
consensus exists for when operative intervention is needed, even 
in the setting of full-thickness injuries. Patient-specific factors 
such as symptom severity, timing with respect to competition, 
and level of play should be considered when designing the treat -
ment plan. Even in the setting of high-grade injuries, nonopera -
tive treatment may be favored because surgical outcomes can be 
uncertain. Studies cite that return to play can be up to 1 year 
after surgery and that over 40% of patients may experience 
chronic problems such as medial pain, recurrent injury, or kine-
siophobia.
35 Therefore surgery should generally be reserved for 
patients who fail a comprehensive nonoperative treatment cycle. 
Many throwing athletes will respond to nonoperative treatment. 
Initial treatment should consist of rest, restoration of elbow 
ROM, strengthening of the dynamic medial stabilizers, and 
optimization of the kinetic chain function (including proper 
hip, spine, and shoulder mobility, and throwing technique). 
Once ROM, strength, and biomechanics are optimal, an inter-
val throwing program should begin ( Box 36.1). Data regarding 
PRP injections are conflicting, but these may be a reasonable 
step for injuries that do not respond to conservative care or  
to reduce pain when the patient is near or in the competitive 
season (so as to avoid a surgery that would end the season).  
If the patient fails to improve with the above, then surgical con -
sultation should be sought.
37,59
A distinct scenario is UCL injury in a nonthrowing athlete. 
Often these occur from a force valgus blow in combat sports or 
football. Because of the acute nature (as opposed to the chronic 
overuse observed in throwers), there is more significant local 
bleeding and therefore more healing; these injuries rarely require 
surgery. Most of these athletes will be able to return to sports 
within 2 to 4 weeks after resolution of symptoms and demonstra-
tion of ability to return safely to movement patterns involved in 
their sport. Initially, treatment should focus on acute pain control 
and restoration of ROM. As athletes demonstrate full strength 
and the ability to protect themselves, they may return to practice 
and competition under supervision. A hinged-elbow brace may be 
a useful tool to provide further protection while they are in the 
recovery phase. It also may reduce fear of reinjury as they return 
for the remainder of the season.
113
Conditions of the Forearm, Wrist, and Hand
de Quervain Tenosynovitis
The first dorsal compartment of the wrist contains the abductor 
pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis tendons. These tendons 
run beneath a sheath over the dorsal aspect of the radial styloid 
process, along the inferior portion of the anatomic snuff box. 
Shear and repetitive microtrauma in this area can result in a teno-
synovitis referred to as de Quervain tenosynovitis.
110 This is the 
most common tendinopathy of the wrist and is frequently seen in 
patients who perform activities requiring gripping and/or repeti -
tive use of the thumb.
22,72
Patients present with gradual onset of pain over the dorsal 
radial aspect of the wrist aggravated by activities such as racquet 
sports, golf, or fishing. Patients can also report a sensation of wrist

to the dorsal radial wrist and tenderness to palpation over the first 
dorsal compartment. The Finkelstein test is pathognomonic for 
the diagnosis.
110 It is performed by placing the patient’s elbow in 
extension, with the forearm in neutral rotation and the wrist in 
radial deviation. The patient should be asked to place the thumb 
in the palm and grip the thumb with the fingers. The examiner 
should then passively deviate the wrist to the ulnar side. Pain  
over the first dorsal compartment is considered a positive test. 
Radiographs are typically not indicated for routine evaluation. 
Diagnostic ultrasound may show fluid in the tendon sheath.
T reatment includes rest, analgesics, and a thumb spica splint. 
Pain refractory to this may benefit from a tendon sheath injection. 
First dorsal compartment peritendinous corticosteroid injection 
has been shown to reduce symptoms in 62% to 100% of cases.
64,134
Scapholunate Instability
Scapholunate instability resulting from ligamentous injury is the 
most common ligamentous injury of the wrist.
78,80 This injury 
occurs after a fall on a pronated, outstretched hand with the 
wrist in extension and ulnar deviation. After scapholunate liga -
ment disruption, the scaphoid moves into a flexed position, 
whereas the lunate and triquetrum become extended. This pat -
tern is referred to as dorsal intercalated segmental instability 
(DISI) pattern (Fig. 36.3).
70 This creates chronic instability of 
the carpus. If this injury is not diagnosed early and treated prop -
erly, the instability leads to progressive posttraumatic arthritis, 
which is termed scapholunate advanced collapse (SLAC)  wrist.
109 
Once a SLAC wrist has developed, there is no corrective treat -
ment, and options are limited to palliative surgical treatment 
such as fusions and/or carpectomy. Therefore it is critical to 
identify and treat this injury early.
Scapholunate instability patients report a FOOSH injury with 
subsequent wrist edema, ecchymosis, and restricted ROM. They 
are tender over the scapholunate joint, particularly on the dorsum 
of the wrist. The patient typically has a positive result on the Wat-
son test, in which shifting of the scaphoid relative to the lunate 
can be felt.
76
Radiographic evaluation should include anteroposterior,  
anteroposterior with a clenched fist, lateral, and oblique radio -
graphs.109 The presence of associated fractures should be assessed.

The lateral radiograph can reveal the presence of a DISI pattern 
injury (see Fig. 36.3). This is characterized by a  scapholunate  
angle of more than 60 degrees. 109 A gap of more than 3 mm  
between the scaphoid and lunate on anteroposterior radiographs 
is also diagnostic for scapholunate instability ( Fig. 36.4).
109 The 
clenched fist view exaggerates this finding. Radiographic evalua-
tion should include comparison studies with the opposite wrist.
If standard radiographs are not diagnostic, an MRI should be 
obtained.
109 Wrist arthroscopy has become the gold standard for 
diagnosing this entity. Acute scapholunate ligament injuries 
should be treated surgically. Chronic scapholunate injuries are 
more difficult to treat but also require surgical intervention.  
Partial wrist arthrodesis can improve chronic scapholunate insta-
bility, and a proximal row carpectomy frequently is used to treat  
advanced scapholunate collapse.
109
Triangular Fibrocartilage Complex Injuries
The triangular fibrocartilage complex (TFCC) is composed of an 
avascular central articular disc and vascular dorsal and palmar 
radioulnar ligaments.
109 The TFCC is the primary stabilizer  
of the distal radioulnar joint and can be injured in an acute  
traumatic event, such as falling on an outstretched hand, or 
through repetitive microtrauma, such as that seen in gymnastics. 
Axial loads to the wrist result in 18% of the load transmitted 
through the TFCC and the remaining 82% through the radio -
carpal joint.
101 A positive ulnar variance (longer ulna than radius 
at the level of the wrist) results in an increase in the load-bearing 
function of the TFCC, which results in a higher incidence of 
TFCC injuries (Fig. 36.5).
101
Patients who sustain a TFCC injury may report either a grad -
ual onset or a single traumatic event. T raumatic tears occur more 
frequently in young athletes, whereas degenerative tears are more 
common in older patients. Patients with acute injuries often  
report an axial load to the wrist associated with rotational stress.
109 
The patient can also report wrist catching. The physical examina-
tion can show tenderness to palpation in the hollow between the 
flexor carpi ulnaris tendon and the extensor carpi ulnaris (ECU) 
tendon, just distal to the ulnar styloid process.
109 This should be 
differentiated from ECU tendinitis, which is more common in 
racquet sports and may have reproducible subluxation on prona -
tion/supination.

Initial treatment should consist of immobilization with a wrist 
splint, rest, pain control measures (NSAIDs or a steroid injec -

preferentially load their wrists). If patients fail to respond ade -

First Metacarpophalangeal Joint Ulnar Collateral 
Ligament Sprain
Radially directed forces across the first metacarpophalangeal 
(MCP) joint can result in UCL injury. This injury occurs after 
injuries such as a fall or impact in which the thumb is forced into 
radial deviation.
110 A fall in downhill skiing is a common cause 
when the gloved thumb is forced into radial deviation by contact 
with the ground. UCL injuries to the first MCP joint can be 
categorized into a three-grade severity scale of ligament sprains 
(see Table 36.2).
When a grade 3 UCL sprain of the first MCP joint occurs as 
a result of avulsion of the distal end of the ligament from the base 
of the first proximal phalanx, there is the possibility of interposi -
tion of the adductor pollicis aponeurosis between the base of the 
first proximal phalanx and the ruptured end of the UCL.
123 This 
is called a Stener lesion and prevents healing of the first MCP UCL 
injury, leading to chronic joint pain and instability.
Patients who sustain first MCP joint UCL injuries report  
a radially directed force across the first MCP joint. They may 
Plus
Neutral
Minus
• Fig. 36.5 Neutral, ulnar minus, and ulnar plus variants.
report an associated “pop” and a feeling of instability in the joint. 
Physical examination reveals tenderness to palpation over the 
UCL. If a Stener lesion is present, a palpable mass on the ulnar 
side of the first MCP joint may be present, representing the 
avulsed UCL.
2 Patients also demonstrate swelling of the first 
MCP , ecchymosis, and increased laxity during radial stress. The 
stress examination should be performed with the joint in both 
full extension and 30 degrees of flexion. A complete tear is indi -
cated by an angular difference between the injured and uninjured 
first MCP joint during stress examination of greater than 15 to 
30 degrees.
54,100 A lack of an endpoint during stress examination 
also suggests complete UCL disruption.
Radiologic evaluation should include anteroposterior, lateral, 
and oblique radiographs to detect the presence of fractures or 
joint subluxation. MRI and diagnostic ultrasound can visualize 
the soft tissue injuries.
T reatment of partial tears involves modalities, analgesics, and 
immobilization for 6 weeks.
39 Patients who participate in contact 
sports should continue to wear a thumb spica splint with a soft 
covering during competition for the remainder of the season. 
Local taping for stability during activity can be used after the 
period of splinting has been completed. Gentle progressive ROM 
exercises should begin after cast immobilization by removing the 
splint twice daily, and activity should progress as tolerated.
Early surgical repair has been advocated for complete ruptures 
of the UCL, particularly if a Stener lesion is present.
34,69,81,99,119 
Surgery is also indicated for individuals with an avulsion fracture 
of the base of the proximal phalanx with angulation and 
displacement greater than 3 mm or with chronic recurrent 
instability.
85 In-season athletes may play in a soft cast with a plan 
for postseason surgical correction. This decision should be made 
in conjunction with the surgical team and with the patient fully 
understanding the risks and benefits of delayed surgery.
Summary
There are many common upper limb injuries and syndromes. 
Although most can be treated nonoperatively, some require surgi-
cal intervention. Accurate diagnosis and initiation of appropriate 
treatment will enhance recovery, and identification of flaws in the 
movement chain can help to optimize rehabilitation and prevent 
recurrent injury.
